did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper models the cost-effectiveness of an Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD) used for the screening, triage, and assessment of skin cancer lesions using dermoscopic images. The study compares AIaMD interventions (including AIaMD and AIaMD with dermatologist second read) against face-to-face and teledermatology assessments for diagnosing melanoma and squamous cell carcinoma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is labeled as an 'Abstract' from a conference supplement (British Journal of Dermatology, Supplement 1). It presents original research involving the development of a cost-utility health economic model (decision tree with Markov models) to compare diagnostic strategies for skin cancer. The study includes specific methods (sensitivity analyses), results (cost savings, QALYs, discharge rates), and conclusions, which fits the definition of a research paper (specifically a conference abstract with original results).

title: Modelling the cost-effectiveness of an artificial intelligence as a medical device
brief_summary: This study developed a cost-utility health economic model to evaluate the cost-effectiveness of an Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD) for screening and triaging skin cancer lesions using dermoscopic images. The decision tree with Markov models compared four diagnostic strategies: face-to-face assessment, teledermatology, AIaMD, and AIaMD with dermatologist second read, against a lifetime horizon of up to 100 years. The analysis demonstrated that AIaMD interventions were more effective and less costly than face-to-face and teledermatology assessments, yielding significant cost savings and quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) benefits. Specifically, the AIaMD with second read was identified as the most cost-effective intervention at a willingness-to-pay threshold of £30,000. The model indicated that AIaMD could reduce total dermatologist workload by 15% and discharge a substantial proportion of benign lesions, particularly pigmented ones. These findings suggest that AIaMD offers a viable strategy to manage rising demand in dermatology services while improving patient outcomes.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the 'high-risk cancer' category for the cost-effectiveness model (BT29). Specifically studied in the melanoma pathway in the AI triage analysis (BT30), where 396 patients were on the melanoma pathway and 116 referrals were discharged by the AI platform.

Included in the 'high-risk cancer' category for the cost-effectiveness model (BT29). Specifically studied in the SCC pathway in the AI triage analysis (BT30), where 404 patients were on the SCC pathway and 32 referrals were discharged by the AI platform.

Included as a distinct diagnostic category '(ii) basal cell carcinoma' in the cost-effectiveness model (BT29). The text notes that BCCs are referred on a separate, routine pathway and were not part of the urgent suspected cancer pathway analyzed in BT30.

Included in the 'noncancerous (benign or precancerous) lesions' category for the cost-effectiveness model (BT29). In the AI triage analysis (BT30), the AI platform was used to screen out benign lesions, particularly benign pigmented lesions, to reduce face-to-face appointments.

Included in the 'high-risk cancer' category '(i) high-risk cancer (including melanoma, squamous cell carcinoma and rare cancers)' for the cost-effectiveness model (BT29). No specific counts or further details provided.

Mentioned in the context of distinguishing benign from malignant pigmented lesions versus keratotic lesions in the AI triage analysis (BT30). The study suspected dermoscopy was more valuable for pigmented lesions than keratotic ones.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs. malignant categorization of skin lesions
all_task_types.details: The AI platform assesses dermoscopic images to categorize lesions into 'benign or malignant' classes. The study evaluates the AI's ability to screen out benign lesions (discharge) versus referring for further assessment, effectively performing binary classification for triage purposes.

datasets_used.name: Retrospective patient cohort from University Hospital Leicester
datasets_used.description: A retrospective review of patients assessed by the Skin Analytics AI platform (DERM) between August 2023 and November 2023. The study utilized data from patients referred by general practitioners on the urgent suspected cancer pathway for suspected melanoma or squamous cell carcinoma.

use.testing: Used to assess the performance of the AI platform in discharging benign lesions and determining the need for further assessment by a trust dermatologist.
use.other: Used to validate the hypothesis that AI is better at recognizing benign pigmented lesions compared to keratotic lesions and to inform commissioning decisions.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify uncertainty in the cost-utility health economic model regarding long-term costs and outcomes.
To quantify uncertainty in the cost-utility health economic model regarding long-term costs and outcomes.
To estimate the long-term costs and outcomes in quality-adjusted life-years (QALYs) of each diagnostic intervention strategy.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing with p-values or significance tests. It utilized a cost-utility health economic model with one-way and probabilistic sensitivity analyses to quantify uncertainty and compared interventions based on cost-effectiveness thresholds (willingness-to-pay) and cost savings.

Retrospective patient cohort from Univer.notes: The AI platform discharged 116 referrals on the melanoma pathway and 32 on the SCC pathway. The study hypothesized and found that AI is better at recognizing benign pigmented lesions, as 92% of patients referred on the SCC pathway required further assessment compared with 70% of those referred on a melanoma pathway.

Cost-utility health economic model (BT29.notes: Cost savings are calculated as £52 per patient, £259,000 for an average trust, or £35 million for NHS England. QALY benefits are due primarily to reduced patient anxiety and biopsies. Capacity modelling showed AIaMD is likely to significantly reduce total dermatologist workload by 15%. Teledermatology reduced the need for face-to-face consultations by > 60%.

Teledermatology.diagnostic_accuracy_comparison: Superior diagnostic accuracy of face-to-face consultations over teledermatology

Teledermatology analysis (BT29 context).notes: Teledermatology provides a 'snapshot' with limited clinical information. The analysis showed most teledermatology referrals were considered adequate, but just over one-third needed to be seen in clinic.

architecture_used.main_model: Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD) platform named DERM (developed by Skin Analytics)

classification_or_output_head.details: The AI platform assesses features of a single dermoscopic image to categorize the lesion into benign or malignant. Information from patient history and macroscopic images is not involved.

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Artificial Intelligence as a Medical Device (AIaMD)
conclusions: The AIaMD (DERM) and AIaMD with dermatologist second read (DERM_SR) interventions are more effective and less costly than face-to-face assessment and teledermatology for screening and triaging skin cancer lesions.
DERM_SR is the most cost-effective intervention at a willingness-to-pay threshold of £30,000, while DERM is the most cost-effective at a threshold of £20,000.
AIaMD interventions yield significant cost savings, estimated at £52 per patient, £259,000 for an average trust, and £35 million for NHS England.
Quality-adjusted life-year (QALY) benefits from AIaMD are primarily due to reduced patient anxiety and fewer biopsies.
AIaMD is likely to significantly reduce total dermatologist workload by 15% compared with face-to-face care.
Teledermatology reduces the need for face-to-face consultations by more than 60%, but face-to-face consultations have superior diagnostic accuracy.

The AI platform is more effective at recognizing benign pigmented lesions than keratotic lesions, as evidenced by higher discharge rates on the melanoma pathway (29.2%) compared to the squamous cell carcinoma pathway (7.9%).
There is parameter uncertainty in the model reflecting a paucity of baseline data, which will be investigated further with refinement of the model as more data becomes available.
important_remarks: Conflict of interest: Three of the five authors are employed by the AI provider (Skin Analytics), which may introduce bias in the study's design or interpretation.
Data limitation: The study acknowledges parameter uncertainty reflecting a paucity of baseline data, noting that the model will require refinement as more data becomes available.

Limited input scope: The AI platform assesses features of a single dermoscopic image only; it does not incorporate patient history or macroscopic images, which dermatologists typically use for diagnosis.

Retrospective design: The AI triage analysis (BT30) is based on a retrospective review of 800 patients from a single center (University Hospital Leicester) between August and November 2023, limiting generalizability.

Lesion type bias: The AI demonstrated significantly higher discharge rates for pigmented lesions (melanoma pathway: 29.2%) compared to keratotic lesions (SCC pathway: 7.9%), suggesting potential performance disparities based on lesion morphology.

Theoretical modeling: The cost-effectiveness results are derived from a decision tree with Markov models with a lifetime horizon, relying on assumptions about long-term costs and QALYs rather than direct clinical trial outcomes.
